changelog:
Fast load balancing reworked into true parallel send-and-ack fan-out (WiFi-7 multi-link), and the in-flight display simplified.

=== Why ===

The "fast" strategy was a 9router-style sticky-round-robin: it pinned a burst of consecutive requests to ONE account (for prompt-cache locality) and rotated only after a sticky cap, relying on a reactive 429 cooldown. Under a parallel burst that serialized work onto a single identity and left peers idle. The request here: spread each request to a DIFFERENT free account in parallel (a "rotate number" per account), so K concurrent requests hit K accounts at once with no per-account queueing; only wait when EVERY account is busy, then route to whichever frees first.

=== Change — parallel-spread fast strategy (pool/account.go) ===

- Replaced sticky-round-robin (PoolStickyLimit, default 3) with PoolFastConcurrency (default 1 = send-and-ack): the per-account concurrent-request cap, i.e. how many requests one account handles at once before the next fans out to a free peer. 1 means strict one-request-per-account; raise it to let an account pipeline N.
- pickFastLocked now selects the LEAST-in-flight eligible account below its cap (tie-break: higher weight, then least-recently-picked via the existing pick-seq), so a same-instant burst rotates across distinct free accounts before any request completes. When reserving and every eligible account is at its cap, it returns the saturation poll hint so the failover dispatcher's existing event-driven admission-wait (ReleaseSignal) blocks until a slot frees and routes there — no request is dropped just because the pool is momentarily full.
- The fast path now routes purely by free capacity: it BYPASSES the soft 429 cooldown (a throttled account is steered around by load, not by a timed skip). Hard-disable is unaffected — a quota-exhausted account without an overage allowance still gets computeEffectiveWeight==0 and is dropped from rotation at the next Reload, so the bypass is bounded. The other strategies (least-request / swr / least-used / random) are unchanged and still honor the cooldown.
- Removed the now-dead GCRA rate pacer / TTFT smart-laning scaffolding from the fast path (the rate_pacer.go module was already deleted); least-request keeps its AIMD concurrency limit, which is now its sole per-account throttle-guard backed by the soft cooldown.

=== Display ===

- The per-account dashboard chip now shows just "In-flight N" while an account is actually serving (dropped the "/ limit" suffix, which only ever meant the least-request AIMD ceiling). EN + ZH hints updated.
- Admin Settings: the "Sticky Limit" input is now "Concurrency / account" (poolFastConcurrency); the Pool Strategy help text describes the fan-out + wait-for-free-slot behavior. GET/POST /admin/api/endpoint carry poolFastConcurrency (0 = default 1); a successful update calls pool.Reload() so it applies mid-run.

=== Safety guard ===

Added a compile-time invariant (proxy/quota.go) asserting the dispatcher's saturation threshold (saturationPollHint) stays >= the pool's saturation interval (now exported as pool.SaturationPollInterval), so a future edit to either constant can't silently break the "wait until an account frees" contract.

=== Tests ===

pool/fast_strategy_test.go rewritten for the fan-out model: fans out across distinct free accounts, saturates at cap with the poll hint, waits-then-routes-to-a-freed-slot, pipelines when cap>1, fill-first by weight, even LRU rotation, and ignores soft cooldown. config/pool_strategy_test.go covers PoolFastConcurrency default (1) + clamp [1,1000]. go build, go vet, go test ./... all green; gofmt clean. Reviewer pass applied (stale rate-pacer doc removed, invariant guard added, local-name shadowing fixed). config.Version + version.json bumped to 1.0.10-A24.

--- 1.0.10-A23 ---

Multi-provider support (Codex / Qoder / any OpenAI-Anthropic-Gemini-compatible endpoint) + 9router-style fast load balancing, with Kiro behavior preserved byte-for-byte.

=== Why ===

Kiro-Go was a single-provider proxy (AWS Kiro only) and its account pool felt slow under light/interactive load. This release turns it into a multi-provider router and fixes the load-balancing latency, modeled on the local source of two reference projects (decolua/9router, Soju06/codex-lb).

=== Load balancing — new "fast" strategy, now the DEFAULT ===

9router selects an account INSTANTLY (fill-first by weight, or sticky-round-robin) and relies purely on a reactive 429 cooldown; no proactive pacing. Kiro-Go's least-request path layered an AIMD concurrency gate + GCRA rate pacer + 750ms admission-wait + TTFT scoring, which added latency under light load. New pool strategy "fast" (pool/account.go): instant pick, no admission stall, configurable sticky cap (PoolStickyLimit, default 3) for prompt-cache locality, pick-time LRU rotation. Made the default; least-request (and swr/least-used/random) remain fully selectable for rollback via admin Settings. A Kiro pool with no other backend behaves identically whether the backend filter is empty or "kiro".

=== Provider abstraction (Kiro path byte-identical) ===

New Provider interface + registry (proxy/provider.go); kiroProvider is a thin wrapper over the existing CallKiroAPIContext / auth.RefreshToken / ListAvailableModels, so a Backend-less account (every existing config.json) resolves to "kiro" and routes exactly as before. config.Account gained a Backend discriminator (empty => kiro) + generic credential fields. The pool filters by backend so a non-Kiro request never lands on a Kiro account; the request reaches a non-Kiro provider via a NormalizedRequest carried on context, so the four large Kiro stream/non-stream handlers keep their exact signatures.

=== Codex (ChatGPT) provider ===

auth/codex_oauth.go: PKCE authorization-code login against auth.openai.com (client_id app_EMoamEEZ73f0CkXaXp7hrann, local callback on the fixed port 1455, code exchange form-encoded + refresh JSON), id_token JWT decode for chatgpt-account-id + plan, plus paste-a-token import. proxy/provider_codex.go calls chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex/responses with Bearer + chatgpt-account-id + a synthesized Codex-CLI fingerprint (stdlib TLS first), parses the Responses SSE into the shared callback, and surfaces usage_limit/capacity refusals as QuotaError so failover cools the account. codex-lb-style routing: a background poll of /backend-api/wham/usage records the 5h/weekly windows and cools an exhausted account until its reset. Admin OAuth start/poll/token endpoints + dashboard "Login with OpenAI" flow.

=== Qoder provider ===

auth/qoder.go: device-token login (openapi.qoder.sh) + the full COSY signing scheme (RSA-PKCS1v15-wrapped AES-128-CBC user-info + MD5 signature over payload||cosyKey||ts||body||sigPath + 17 Cosy-* headers) and the WAF-bypass body encoding (base64 -> thirds-rearrange -> custom-alphabet substitution), ported faithfully and cross-checked against an independent reference implementation in the test. proxy/provider_qoder.go builds the bespoke chat payload, encodes + signs it, and unwraps Qoder's {statusCodeValue, body} SSE envelope. Admin device-login start/poll endpoints + dashboard flow.

=== Data-driven OpenAI / Anthropic / Gemini providers + add-account UX ===

proxy/provider_catalog.go ports 9router's PROVIDERS map (Groq, Cerebras, DeepSeek, Mistral, OpenRouter, Together, Fireworks, Cohere, Nebius, SiliconFlow, xAI, Anthropic, Gemini, +more) — OpenAI-compatible ones are data-only, served by one genericProvider keyed on dialect. proxy/translate_{openai,anthropic,gemini}.go translate Claude<->OpenAI<->Gemini and parse each dialect's SSE into the shared callback; proxy/model_resolve.go routes a "provider/model" (or alias) prefix to the right backend.

The generic provider now FETCHES the live model catalog from {base}/models on add (parsing OpenAI data/models/results/bare-array shapes) instead of returning a static list — so adding your own OpenAI-compatible endpoint actually gets its models. A new "Custom (OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini-compatible)" add path takes a Display Name + API BASE URL (e.g. https://api.example.com/v1, NOT a /chat/completions URL) + key + dialect, registers a provider on the fly, and reports the fetched model count; /chat/completions and /models are both derived from the base. Critical routing fix: the pool's per-account model filter matches the DE-PREFIXED upstream id for non-Kiro backends across all four paths, otherwise a fetched catalog would shed prefixed requests as "no available accounts". The dashboard account list shows a backend badge + model count for provider accounts.

=== Kiro endpoint change (firewall allowlist) ===

Per Kiro's published firewall allowlist (runtime.<region>.kiro.dev is the active "Kiro service" inference host; q.<region>.amazonaws.com is documented legacy), the INFERENCE streaming chain now leads with runtime.<region>.kiro.dev as PRIMARY in every region (including us-east-1), keeping q.* and codewhisperer.* as legacy fallback (not removed). The account-MANAGEMENT REST base stays on codewhisperer.us-east-1.amazonaws.com for us-east-1 — the runtime host serves only the inference action, and pointing the management paths at it breaks account refresh (no REST fallback chain). sortRegionalEndpoints resolves the preferred endpoint by name rather than a fragile index.

=== Review / verification ===

Reviewer + security-audit passes; both blockers fixed (a data race in the Codex callback shutdown closed with sync.Once; the Responses/WS paths threading the request to non-Kiro providers) plus https-only SSRF guards on custom base URLs, constant-time OAuth state compare, and callback-server timeouts. Also fixed a long-standing flaky cooldown test. go build, go vet, go test ./... all green across all packages; gofmt clean. -race needs cgo (CI runs it on Linux); the closed race is fixed by construction. config.Version + version.json bumped to 1.0.10-A23.

--- 1.0.10-A22 ---

Latency: tune TTFT-aware routing to the live pool + add per-request timing instrumentation.

=== Why ===

Measured the live proxy from outside (curl connect/ttfb/total). Findings: proxy+network overhead is ~40ms (/health) — negligible. The 8000ms "TTFT" on the dashboard is almost entirely UPSTREAM first-token time, and it scales with request size: a 512B request returns first token in ~2s, a 25KB Claude-Code-scale request (15 tools + big system prompt) in 3-10s. Thinking on/off barely moved it (7.8s vs 9.8s); prompt-cache repeat did not help (3.6-11.8s, high variance). Crucially, the SAME pool showed a 6.4x TTFT spread across accounts (1.5s fastest vs 9.6s slowest) — so routing to the fast identity is the real proxy-side lever.

=== Change (pool/rate_pacer.go) ===

- ttftPenaltyCap 4 -> 10. The latency-aware scorer divides an account's least-request score by min(ttft/minTTFT, cap). At cap=4 a 6.4x-slower account was only penalized 4x, so it still drew meaningful traffic onto the slow lane. Cap=10 lets the penalty track the measured ratio up to an order of magnitude. Still a strong PREFERENCE, not a ban: even at 10x an account keeps ~10% weight so a recovered account is re-probed; unmeasured accounts stay at factor 1.0 to explore.
- ttftEWMAAlpha 0.3 -> 0.5. TTFT swings widely request-to-request, so weight the newest sample equally with history; a slowing or recovering account is reflected in ~2 requests instead of ~4. Outliers are still bounded by ttftMaxSampleMs and the penalty cap.

=== Instrumentation (proxy/kiro.go) ===

- KIRO_TIMING=1 (env-gated, off by default, zero hot-path cost) logs one line per request decomposing first-token latency: firstTokenMs, headersMs (dispatch->response headers = connect/HTTP-layer), genGapMs (firstToken-headers = pure upstream model latency), endpointsTried, throttled, endpoint. Per-request state is LOCAL (no cross-request races). Lets an operator see upstream-vs-proxy split from inside.

=== Scope (honest) ===

This shaves the SLOW-ACCOUNT TAIL by concentrating traffic on whichever identity is currently fast. It does NOT lower the floor — the core 2-8s is AWS upstream first-token time scaling with request size, which no proxy change can shrink. Healthier/under-quota accounts remain the fix for the floor; smaller prompts (e.g. re-enabling FilterEnvNoise, disabling unused webSearch) also help upstream latency.

=== Tests ===

pool/least_request_test.go: TestTTFTRoutingPenaltyCap (a 6.4x-slower account that would win at cap=4 now loses to the fast account at cap=10) and TestTTFTRoutingExploresUnmeasured (an unmeasured account is not penalized, still gets traffic to learn its TTFT). go build, go vet, go test ./... all green. version.json + config.Version bumped to 1.0.10-A22.--- 1.0.10-A21 ---

Fix dropped tool-use on stream EOS — the "preamble then ends the turn" bug.

=== Why ===

A tool call (function call) whose stop:true frame never arrived — because the upstream connection dropped between the tool-input frames and the terminator — was SILENTLY DISCARDED at clean EOF. parseEventStream only ever delivered a tool use via finishToolUse on stop:true (or when a different toolUseId arrived); on EOF the pending currentToolUse was abandoned, the loop fell through to OnComplete, the handler saw no tool call, and the request resolved to a fabricated end_turn. A client like Claude Code therefore emitted its "Let me look at the project structure..." preamble and then ENDED THE TURN instead of running the tool — repeatedly, mid-task.

This is AWS's documented "UnexpectedToolUseEos" shape: "the stream can unexpectedly end while waiting for an exceptionally complex tool use [because] some proxy server dropped the idle connection after a timeout." It is distinct from the A20 mid-frame byte-truncation fix — here whole frames arrived fine, but the logical tool use was incomplete.

=== Change (proxy/kiro.go) ===

On clean EOF with a tool use still being assembled, decide by the completeness of the accumulated input (correct whether stop:true is a guaranteed terminator or best-effort):
- empty input (argless tool) OR input that parses as valid complete JSON -> the call finished and only the stop frame was lost in transit; FLUSH it via finishToolUse so the client actually runs the tool.
- non-empty input that is NOT valid JSON -> genuinely truncated mid-arguments; we cannot fabricate the rest, so return classifyStreamError(io.ErrUnexpectedEOF) -> a retryable *ErrUpstreamStreamReset. The dispatcher fails over to a peer account pre-commit (clean re-run), or the post-commit path emits a real error frame. Never a fabricated end_turn, never a tool call with garbage args.

A proxy can do strictly better than AWS's own Q CLI here, which salvages a malformed partial with a warning because it is the terminal consumer — we re-run cleanly on another account instead.

=== Tests ===

proxy/stream_truncation_test.go: pending tool use with valid JSON is flushed on EOF (Glob with complete args); pending tool use cut mid-arguments (invalid partial JSON) is a retryable *ErrUpstreamStreamReset and is NOT flushed with partial input; an argless pending tool use is flushed; a complete stop:true tool stream still succeeds. go build, go vet, go test ./... all green.

Research basis (confirmed against source): aws/amazon-q-developer-cli chat-cli parser.rs — stop:true terminates a tool use (if let Some(true) = stop { break }), input accumulates incrementally (tool_string.push_str(&i)), and UnexpectedToolUseEos is raised when the stream ends mid-tool-use. version.json + config.Version bumped to 1.0.10-A21.
--- 1.0.10-A20 ---

Streaming fidelity fixes — verbatim incremental deltas + mid-frame truncation detection.

=== Why ===

Two user-visible streaming defects, root-caused against AWS's own Amazon Q CLI source and three reference proxies (not guesswork):

1. Character/space drops in streamed text ("water" -> "wate", "i like you" -> "ilike you", a doubled word collapsing to one). The Kiro/CodeWhisperer generateAssistantResponse stream is INCREMENTAL: each assistantResponseEvent.content is a delta fragment and the full message is their verbatim concatenation (AWS's own Q CLI does a plain push_str; every reference proxy does result.content += event.content). The proxy's normalizeChunk deduper wrongly assumed CUMULATIVE snapshots and silently dropped legitimate text: the HasPrefix(chunk, prev) branch ate characters across fragment boundaries, and the chunk==prev branch dropped legitimately-repeated tokens. That dedup has no basis in any reference implementation.

2. Claude Code "suddenly ends the turn" on a dropped connection. CodeWhisperer/Q has NO application-level terminal event (no messageStop/stopReason/finishReason) — a complete response is signaled by a clean frame-boundary EOF, which is indistinguishable from a boundary-aligned connection drop even to AWS's own client. But a connection cut MID-FRAME is detectable.

=== Change ===

- Removed normalizeChunk entirely; parseEventStream now forwards every assistantResponseEvent / reasoningContentEvent content fragment VERBATIM. The binary event-stream frame parser already delivers each frame exactly once, so there is nothing to dedup — the dedup only ever corrupted legitimate output.
- classifyStreamError now treats io.ErrUnexpectedEOF (a mid-frame cut — io.ReadFull short-reads the 12-byte prelude or the frame body) as a retryable *ErrUpstreamStreamReset: the dispatcher fails over to a peer account pre-commit, and post-commit the client gets the friendly stream-reset error instead of a fabricated end_turn. A clean frame-boundary EOF remains a normal successful completion (correct per the upstream contract).
- No change to the HTTP/2 keepalive/ping layer (A7/A18) — investigation confirmed the char-drop was application-level delta handling, not transport.

=== Tests ===

- proxy/kiro_test.go: TestStreamConcatenatesIncrementalDeltasVerbatim drives fragment sequences through the real parseEventStream and asserts verbatim concatenation, reproducing the exact reported bugs ("wat"+"er"->"water", "i"+" like"+" you"->"i like you"), plus repeated tokens, boundary spaces, prefix-extension, and unicode boundaries.
- proxy/stream_truncation_test.go: clean frame-boundary EOF is success; mid-prelude and mid-body truncation classify as retryable *ErrUpstreamStreamReset; a complete tool-use stream is not flagged.

go build, go vet, go test ./... all green. Research basis: AWS amazon-q-developer-cli (chat-cli parser.rs push_str + Ok(None) end-of-stream; the ChatResponseStream union has no stop event), jwadow/kiro-gateway, aliom-v/KiroGate, caidaoli/kiro2api. version.json and config.Version both bumped to 1.0.10-A20.
--- 1.0.10-A19 ---

Fast adaptive concurrency for least-request — slow-start ramp + remembered ceiling (ssthresh), tunable from the admin UI.

=== Why ===

The least-request concurrency limit opened too slowly: a fresh/recovered account started at a low window and grew additively, so it sat throttled below what the AWS identity could actually serve for tens of seconds. A16/A18 added the GCRA rate pacer as the proactive 429-guard; this change makes the concurrency limit (the burst gate that sits alongside the pacer) ramp FAST without re-introducing storms.

=== Change — TCP-style control loop (pool/account.go) ===

- SLOW-START below a remembered ceiling: on an at-capacity success the limit grows MULTIPLICATIVELY (limit += limit/2), reaching the ceiling in a handful of successes instead of ~tens of additive steps.
- CONGESTION-AVOIDANCE at/above ssthresh: grows +1 (gentle probing) so it does not slam back into the bucket.
- REMEMBERED CEILING (ssthresh): on a 429 the limit backs off to floor(limit x 3/4) AND records that point as ssthresh (CUBIC/Reno fast-recovery), so recovery snaps back near the known-good ceiling instead of re-climbing from the floor.
- Coexists with the A16/A18 rate pacer + TTFT smart-laning unchanged: the pacer remains the proactive rate guard, so the faster concurrency climb does not itself induce throttling. Kept main's relaxed inflight>=limit-1 growth gate and the 3-slot floor.

=== Tunable from the admin UI ===

- New poolInitialConcurrency / poolMaxConcurrency config (0 = built-in default 12 / 48). Clamped to [1,256] on read; max floored to >= initial. GetPoolInitialConcurrency / GetPoolMaxConcurrency / UpdatePoolConcurrency mirror the GetPoolStrategy nil/default pattern; the pool caches the bounds on Reload so the request hot path never nests the config lock under the pool lock.
- GET/POST /admin/api/endpoint carry the two values; an inverted or out-of-range range is rejected with 400. A successful update calls pool.Reload() so the change applies mid-run without a restart.
- admin Settings -> Pool Strategy section gains two number inputs (Initial / Max Concurrency per account) with inline help; empty = default.

Research basis: RFC 5681/6928, AWS SDK adaptive retry (CUBIC), Netflix concurrency-limits, Google SRE Handling Overload, P2C/peak-EWMA.

Tests: slow-start reaches max in a few saturated successes, ssthresh memory switches to additive probing at the ceiling, configured bounds honored; config validation (defaults, clamping, max>=initial, UpdatePoolConcurrency). go build, go vet, go test ./... all green. Rebased onto A18; version A19 (config.Version + version.json).
--- 1.0.10-A18 ---

HTTP/2 connection-lost hardening + dashboard realtime + load-balancer Phase-A speed tuning.

=== HTTP/2 "client connection lost" (fixes the API Error users saw) ===

Three gaps behind "API Error: http2: client connection lost":
- classifyStreamError did not recognize the string "http2: client connection lost" (created inline in http2.closeForLostPing, NOT an exported sentinel — string match is the only option). It matched only INTERNAL_ERROR/stream error/RST_STREAM/GOAWAY, so post-commit the raw Go string leaked straight to the client. Now caught as a retryable stream-reset.
- The 15s ReadIdleTimeout + 15s PingTimeout were more aggressive than gRPC's own 20s keepalive ack default. A PING ack delayed by an alive-but-busy intermediary (AWS ALB/NLB, NAT, TLS proxy) during a legitimate quiet stretch tripped closeForLostPing and aborted the in-flight request — a false-positive teardown. Relaxed to 30s read-idle + 20s ping-timeout (50s detection budget, still far under the 5m idle cap enforced by test).
- Two Responses paths (Codex SSE + WS) leaked any raw upstream error post-commit. New safeStreamErrorMessage helper (one owner of redaction) is now used at every post-commit error-emit site (Claude SSE, Responses SSE, Responses WS): the real cause (ARNs, hosts, raw http2 strings) goes to the operator WARN log, the client only ever sees a stable friendly message. New regression tests TestClassifyStreamError_ClientConnectionLost and TestSafeStreamErrorMessageNeverLeaks.

=== Dashboard realtime + login flash ===

- inflight (and cooldown countdown, paced rate, AIMD limit) were not realtime: broadcasts only fired on request COMPLETION (after the in-flight slot was already released), and the 30s timer sent only WS pings. A new shared dashboardPusher goroutine broadcasts a fresh snapshot ~1s while >=1 dashboard is connected (gated by hasSubscribers() so it is a free no-op when nobody is watching), torn down via stopDashboardPusher in Stop(). Every live-only field is now realtime.
- Login page flashed ~0.1s on refresh: the login page was painted visible by default and the swap to the dashboard happened after first paint. A pre-paint inline <head> script now sets a pre-authed class on <html> when a fresh localStorage session exists, hiding the login page before first paint; showLogin() clears the class on verify-failure so a revoked session is never trapped on the dashboard.
- Other stale indicators fixed: Overview trend sparklines + top-models now refresh on the 5s active-tab poll; the Analytics per-key breakdown now re-fetches its key totals (loadApiKeysData split out of loadApiKeys).
- Hardened Stop() to be nil-channel-safe (closeOnce helper) so a test-constructed Handler literal can call Stop() without a close(nil) panic.

=== Load balancer Phase A — speed tuning (balanced ~90-95% run-hot) ===

First half of a two-phase load-balancer rework. The A16 pacing was tuned hard for 429-avoidance, which left throughput on the table and felt slow — especially the ~43s recovery after a single 429. Phase A loosens the constants toward a balanced run-hot target; Phase B (separate) does the architectural rework (lock removal, event-driven admission, multi-signal scoring, warm H2).
- Rate snap-down on a 429: ×0.5 -> ×0.85 (rate_pacer.go) — lands at ~90% of the discovered limit instead of halving.
- Rate probe-up: +5%/3s -> +15%/1.5s — recovery after a 429 drops from ~43s to ~2-4s.
- Burst tolerance ~2-deep -> ~3-deep; observed-rate EWMA 0.3 -> 0.5 so the snap-down is seeded from a current estimate, not a lagging-low one.
- AIMD concurrency init/floor/cap 2/2/12 -> 4/3/32 (a 2-account pool now allows up to ~64 concurrent vs 24); growth +1 -> +2 with the gate relaxed to inflight>=limit-1, so the ceiling is reached in half the successful requests.
- Admission saturation poll 100ms -> 25ms (a freed slot is reused ~4x sooner). Phase B replaces this poll with an event-driven wakeup on Release.
- All test assertions on the old constants updated to the new intended behavior.

=== Tests ===

go build, go vet, go test ./... all green; dashboard inline scripts parse clean. Phase A directly fixes recovery slowness and the small-pool concurrency ceiling; the larger fan-out latency (single selection lock + polling admission) is addressed in Phase B.

--- 1.0.10-A17 ---
Dashboard realtime polish + login-on-refresh fix + dead-code cleanup.

=== Login no longer flashes on refresh ===

On every page load the login page was the default-visible element and tryAutoLogin() only swapped to the dashboard AFTER an async /admin/api/status round-trip, so each refresh flashed the login screen (and lingered if the check was slow). Now a saved, non-expired password restores the dashboard OPTIMISTICALLY and verification runs in the background — only an actual rejection (revoked/changed password) falls back to login; a transient network blip keeps the dashboard up and lets the WS + poll fallback recover.

=== Realtime on every tab ===

Overview and Accounts already updated live via the status WebSocket (renderStatusFromObject + mergeAccountStats). API Keys and Analytics were fetched once on tab-open and went stale until a manual refresh. Added a light 5s live-refresh of whichever of those two tabs is currently visible — guarded so it skips while a modal is open and while the browser tab is backgrounded, and it only re-fetches the active tab so the WS broadcast snapshot stays small. Net effect: every tab now updates on its own, no manual refresh.

=== Dead-code cleanup ===

renderAccounts() and switchTab() were each DEFINED TWICE; the later definitions won and the earlier copies were dead code from a prior UI iteration. Removed the two dead blocks (the stale .tab-based switchTab and the older renderAccounts without the in-flight/paced chips). One definition of each remains; the live nav/render paths are unchanged.

=== Notes ===

Dashboard is served from disk, so these take effect on a hard browser reload (Ctrl+Shift+R) — no rebuild needed. Pure web/index.html change; the Go side is untouched. Inline dashboard script re-parsed clean; go build green.

On concurrency (asked alongside): the proxy accepts effectively unlimited concurrent requests (one goroutine per request, no global cap), and least-request selection + the per-account AIMD concurrency limit (init 2, cap 12) + the 750ms admission-wait/shed + the A16 rate pacer SHAPE that burst across the pool rather than rejecting it — smooth interleaving with a clean Retry-After only past real capacity. The throughput ceiling is the account count, not the proxy; adding accounts is linear extra capacity the balancer spreads automatically.

--- 1.0.10-A16 ---
Proactive per-account RATE pacing (GCRA + AIMD rate learning) + security/concurrency audit fixes.

=== Headline: rate pacing so a small pool (even 2 accounts) stops bouncing off 429s ===

The pool already balanced on CONCURRENCY (least-request selection + an AIMD in-flight limit). But each account is an AWS identity throttled by a hidden per-identity TOKEN BUCKET — a RATE limit (tokens/sec), not a concurrency limit. Two fast back-to-back requests each spend a token without ever raising in-flight, so the concurrency gate never saw them; with only 2 accounts you cannot spread that load and you bounce off 429s no matter how good the selection is.

A16 adds the missing Tier-1 mechanism from the load-balancing literature: a GCRA (Generic Cell Rate Algorithm) pacer per account whose rate is LEARNED by AIMD, in a "discover then pace" stance:
- Starts UNPACED (full speed, gated only by concurrency) so cold start is as fast as possible; achieved throughput is measured continuously (EWMA).
- On the first 429 AFTER throughput was measured, the paced rate snaps to half the observed throughput (AIMD multiplicative-decrease) and GCRA pacing engages — the account comes back paced, not just cooled, so it does not immediately re-trip.
- On sustained success the paced rate probes back up +5% every 3s (AIMD additive-increase), sawtoothing just below the real ceiling so no throughput is left on the table.
- A 429 before any measurement leaves the account unpaced (pre-A16 behavior) — we never guess a rate we never observed.
- Per-account GCRA phase offset staggers the two pacers so a synchronized client burst does not drain both buckets at the same instant (the dominant small-pool failure mode). When all accounts are momentarily paced, the existing 750ms admission-wait turns it into a short smooth delay instead of a 429.

Gated to the least-request strategy only (same as the concurrency AIMD); swr/random/least-used are byte-for-byte unchanged and the feature is fully reversible by switching strategy. New pool/rate_pacer.go holds the pure GCRA + AIMD-rate helpers; pool/rate_pacer_test.go covers GCRA admit/advance, rate snap-down/probe-up, decay survival, and burst gating. Two real bugs fixed while wiring it: decayCountersLocked and RecordSuccess were both deleting the cooldown entry that holds the learned rate + throughput measurement (a decay-period sawtooth and a measurement reset between every pair of successes). The learned rate now survives; the dashboard shows a live "Paced X/s" chip (EN + ZH). research basis: Mitzenmacher P2C, Google SRE adaptive throttling, AWS exponential-backoff-and-jitter, Netflix concurrency-limits (AIMD), GCRA/token-bucket, Stripe/GitHub/Shopify rate-limit writeups.

=== Security & concurrency audit fixes (2 Major, 10 Minor, nits) ===

MAJOR:
- SSRF (validateProxyURL): a per-account proxyURL hostname was never resolved, so the link-local guard only caught literal IPs — a name resolving to 169.254.169.254 (cloud metadata) slipped through and could route the AWS bearer token to an attacker. Now resolves the host and rejects any resolved link-local address (best-effort: an unresolvable host is still allowed, preserving the prior permissive behavior; DNS-rebind is a dial-time concern).
- 401/403 cross-account failover (isRetryableUpstreamError): auth errors were terminal for the whole dispatcher even though tokens are per-account and failover picks a DIFFERENT account. The dead account is already disabled + excluded, so 401/403 is now retryable across accounts (rotates to a healthy peer instead of 503-ing); 402/OVERAGE stays terminal.

MINOR:
- Agentic-loop (web/tool search) round failures double-counted global request stats; added a non-counting failover variant so the loop owns once-per-request accounting.
- Client disconnect now cancels the upstream call: CallKiroAPIContext threads r.Context() through all streaming handlers + the Responses WS path, so an abandoned request stops burning credits and frees its AIMD slot instead of running to the 5-min idle timeout.
- Dashboard WS no longer echoes the admin password in the 101 Sec-WebSocket-Protocol response header (it landed in reverse-proxy logs / DevTools); a static "kiro-admin-v1" token is echoed instead.
- Upstream AWS error strings (ARNs, endpoints, account ids) are no longer surfaced to clients; safeUpstreamError logs the real cause server-side and returns a generic message.
- Full conversation payloads are no longer dumped at DEBUG by default (privacy/retention); a byte-count summary is logged unless KIRO_DUMP_PAYLOADS=1.
- Responses WebSocket path now passes through the global rate-limit gate before upgrade.
- Admin brute-force map gets a periodic background sweeper so a distributed single-attempt attack from many IPs cannot grow it unbounded.
- UpdateAccountStats is called synchronously (it only sets a dirty flag) instead of in an unrecovered goroutine that could crash the process on panic.
- OpenAI streaming path got the same downstream SSE heartbeat as the Claude path (with write-mutex serialization) so a long quiet generation is not seen as a dead connection.
- Responses HTTP + WS paths now reserve/release an AIMD concurrency slot, so Codex traffic participates in the per-account concurrency gate.

NITS: generateAPIKeyID handles a crypto/rand error with a unique fallback; the CallKiroAPI success path defers Close/cancel so a callback panic cannot leak the connection; the Windows os.Chmod(0600) limitation is documented at the config-write site.

=== Tests ===

go build, go vet, go test ./... all green; dashboard inline script parses clean. New: pool/rate_pacer_test.go; updated failover/tool-search tests for the new contracts. Validation is unit-level — the rate pacer should be confirmed under a live parallel-agent burst against the real account pool before being relied on in production (watch the new Paced chip: it should appear after the first 429 and then climb, not flap).

--- 1.0.10-A15 ---
Tool-search emulation now defaults OFF — fixes "model narrates an action then ends the turn without calling any tool" for Claude Code clients running with ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=true.

=== Symptom ===

Through the proxy, an agentic turn would return assistant text like 'I'll read the file now' and then stop (stop_reason end_turn) with no tool_use block — so nothing actually happened. It looked like the relay 'suddenly stopped working' even though no code or config had changed.

=== Root cause ===

Claude Code with ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=true marks most tools defer_loading:true and adds a tool_search_tool_regex/bm25 server tool. Our proxy-side Tool Search emulation (added in A3/A10, config default ON) then withheld every real tool schema from the upstream CodeWhisperer/Kiro model and exposed a single synthetic tool_search function it had to call FIRST to reveal its tools (proxy/tool_search_loop.go). Kiro models don't reliably perform that indirection: they narrate an action and end the turn without calling tool_search, so their real tools never become visible and no tool_use is emitted — the loop then returns the narration with end_turn (tool_search_loop.go:157-160). This matches Anthropic's own guidance that tool search auto-disables on a non-first-party ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL 'because most proxies do not forward tool_reference blocks'; forcing it on through a proxy is what triggered the path. Confirmed against both sibling relays in-tree (kiro2api, kiro-gateway) — neither emulates tool search; both forward all tools eagerly.

=== Fix ===

GetToolSearchEnabled() now defaults FALSE (config/config.go). A nil ToolSearchEnabled (fresh install or a config predating an explicit toggle) is treated as OFF. With the emulation off, a tool_search request falls through to the normal handler path, which drops the unsupported tool_search server tool (it carries a Type, so isAnthropicServerTool catches it in convertClaudeTools) and forwards every real tool eagerly with defer_loading ignored — so the model sees all its tools and calls them directly via normal tool_use, exactly like the sibling relays and like Claude Code's own non-first-party fallback. The streaming + failover path is the battle-tested one, restoring live token streaming for these requests too.

Tradeoff: requests no longer get the tool-definition token savings defer_loading was designed for. For a CodeWhisperer/Kiro backend that cannot round-trip tool_reference blocks, reliability is the correct trade. Operators who want to re-enable the emulation can set toolSearchEnabled:true in config / admin Settings (opt-in).

No client-side change is required — but a Claude Code user can also stop forcing the feature by removing ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=true from settings.json, which makes Claude Code load tools upfront on a non-first-party base URL on its own.

=== Tests ===

config/tool_search_test.go rewritten to assert default-OFF semantics (nil -> false, explicit true opts in, explicit false stays off). go build, go vet, go test ./... all green. The tool_search.go pure detection/search/shaping unit tests are unchanged and still pass (the emulation code remains correct and available behind the opt-in toggle).

--- 1.0.10-A14 ---
Upstream-parity fixes: real-AWS overage visibility/control, 1M context-window classification, stateless previous_response_id honesty, and immediate request-path account disable.

=== Why ===

A gap analysis against upstream Quorinex/Kiro-Go (1.1.2) surfaced four reachable issues. This release closes them, lowest-risk to highest, while keeping every place our fork is already ahead.

=== 1) Context-window misclassification (real bug) ===

getContextWindowSize only matched a fixed '4.6'/'4.7' substring list, so Claude 4.8/4.9/5.x — which our model router DOES resolve — fell to 200K instead of their real 1M window, under-reporting input tokens ~5x and mistiming client compaction. Replaced with a regexp-free version parser (isLargeContextModel / parseClaudeVersion): Claude >=4.6 (and any major >=5) report 1M, with a substring fallback for non-standard ids. proxy/kiro.go.

=== 2) previous_response_id silent context loss ===

We are a stateless passthrough (no server-side Responses store), but /v1/responses accepted previous_response_id and silently dropped it, so the model lost all prior context with no signal. Now both the HTTP and WebSocket Responses paths reject it with an explicit 400 invalid_request_error telling the caller to resend full input each turn. proxy/responses_handler.go, proxy/responses_ws.go.

=== 3) Immediate request-path account disable ===

A suspended (TEMPORARILY_SUSPENDED) or auth-dead (401/403, invalid/expired token, invalid_grant) account was only disabled on the next background RefreshAccountInfo tick, so the picker could keep selecting a dead account until then. recordPoolError now classifies these terminal conditions (isSuspensionErrorMessage / isAuthErrorMessage) and disables the account on the failing request (disableAccountByID: BanStatus=BANNED, Enabled=false, pool reload), idempotently to avoid Save/Reload thrash. The existing background refresh still auto-clears BanStatus back to ACTIVE on a later healthy refresh, so a transient blip self-heals. proxy/quota.go.

=== 4) Real-AWS overage visibility + control (the headline) ===

Ported upstream's real overage subsystem as an ADDITIVE feature; our local AllowOverage/OverageWeight routing is unchanged.
- New proxy/kiro_overage.go: FetchOverageStatus reads the REAL AWS user-level Overages switch state + live billing figures (overageCap / overageRate / currentOverages USD) from GET /getUsageLimits; SetOverageStatus flips the actual AWS billing switch via POST /setUserPreference then re-fetches; PersistOverageSnapshot caches it. All region-pinned to the account's stable region (A13 resolveAccountRegion) so an identity's overage calls match its inference region.
- config: six additive cache fields on Account (OverageStatus/Capability/Cap/Rate/CurrentOverages/CheckedAt) + UpdateAccountOverageStatus persister. AllowOverage/OverageWeight retained for routing; the AWS fields are informational cache, not a replacement.
- Admin API: GET /admin/api/accounts/{id}/overage (read+sync, read-only) and POST .../overage (flip the real switch). Enabling requires confirm=true since it authorizes real overage billing. Account JSON now exposes the overage fields.
- Dashboard: account detail panel gains a 'AWS Overage Switch (real billing)' tile showing status + accrued/cap/rate USD + last-synced, with Refresh-from-AWS and Enable/Disable buttons (enable double-confirms). Full EN/ZH i18n.
This lets the dashboard SEE accumulated overage dollars (warn before a surprise bill) and toggle the real AWS switch, instead of only gating routing locally.

=== Tests ===

proxy/context_window_test.go (4.6-5.x classification incl. double-digit minor + version parser), proxy/account_disable_test.go (auth/suspension classifiers, disjoint from quota/429), proxy/kiro_overage_test.go (snapshot parse, UNKNOWN fallback, nil guard, persister round-trip not clobbering AllowOverage). go build, go vet, go test ./... all green.

Note: this release deliberately does NOT port upstream's Responses disk store / previous_response_id chaining (only relevant for stateful Responses clients) — we reject the field honestly instead. Everywhere our fork already leads (idle reader, HTTP/2 pings, RST recovery, AIMD least-request, region pinning, web/tool search, graded reasoning effort, rich per-key quotas, realtime dashboard) is unchanged.

--- Per-account region pinning, HTTP/2 stream-reset recovery, and faster concurrency recovery.

=== Why ===

A12's adaptive load balancing curbed 429 storms but left three rough edges under real use: (1) a low concurrency floor stalled a just-recovered account, (2) an upstream HTTP/2 RST_STREAM (INTERNAL_ERROR) killed the whole request and leaked the raw Go error to the client, and (3) an attempt to spread load by rotating the per-request endpoint/region made a single OAuth identity hop service-actions and regions in a way no real Kiro IDE client does. This release fixes the first two cleanly and replaces the third with a safe, stable design.

=== Endpoint/region: spread ACROSS accounts, never WITHIN one identity ===

- Reverted the per-request endpoint-action rotation introduced mid-A12 development (a process-global counter that round-robined X-Amz-Target across Kiro IDE / CodeWhisperer / AmazonQ on every healthy request). A healthy request again uses the primary action in declared order, with the other two as REACTIVE 429-only fallback within the same region — one consistent service action per request.
- New per-account stable region pinning (proxy/kiro.go resolveAccountRegion): each account resolves to exactly ONE region for its whole life. Precedence: KIRO_API_REGIONS picked deterministically by crc32(accountID) % N -> global KIRO_API_REGION -> us-east-1. Different accounts spread across the configured regions (distributing load over regional rate buckets) while no single account ever changes region. getSortedEndpoints now takes the account and builds the chain for that account's pinned region only; one identity never crosses regions (no impossible-travel pattern). This replaced the old KIRO_API_REGIONS = per-request cross-region failover chain.
- REST consistency: new kiroRestBaseForAccount pins each account's usage / userinfo / models / profiles lookups to the SAME region as its streaming traffic, so an identity's REST and inference calls never split across regions.
- Connection reuse win: because each account now sticks to one host, keep-alive connections are actually reused instead of paying a fresh TLS handshake per request (the reverted rotation was alternating hosts every call and defeating reuse — a real latency cost).

=== Concurrency recovery (faster, fewer false sheds) ===

- aimdMinLimit raised 1 -> 2 (pool/account.go): a just-recovered account keeps 2 concurrent slots instead of being throttled to a single probe, so it returns to useful throughput immediately under a small burst while still self-tuning down on real 429s.
- admissionWaitBudget lowered 2s -> 750ms (proxy/failover.go): when every eligible account is momentarily saturated, the dispatcher sheds with Retry-After ~2.7x faster instead of stalling the client for up to 2s.

=== HTTP/2 stream-reset recovery ===

- New proxy/stream_reset.go: classifyStreamError wraps upstream HTTP/2 RST_STREAM / GOAWAY (typed *http2.StreamError / *http2.GoAwayError, plus an INTERNAL_ERROR / 'stream error' / RST_STREAM / GOAWAY substring fallback) in an *ErrUpstreamStreamReset sentinel. Applied at the transport Do() site and every parseEventStream read-failure return.
- isRetryableUpstreamError now treats the sentinel as retryable, so a transient peer RST rotates to another account (pre-commit) instead of failing the request. context.Canceled / DeadlineExceeded stay non-retryable.
- Post-commit (already streaming, can't fail over) the inline SSE error is now a clean overloaded_error with message 'upstream stream reset (INTERNAL_ERROR); please retry' instead of leaking 'stream error: stream ID N; INTERNAL_ERROR; received from peer'; the raw cause is logged at WARN for operators.

=== Tests ===

- proxy/stream_reset_test.go: typed StreamError + GoAwayError classification, substring fallback, non-reset pass-through, retryable classification incl. the string variant, context-cancel stays non-retryable, the client-facing message doesn't leak 'received from peer', and a stub-transport end-to-end CallKiroAPI returning *ErrUpstreamStreamReset.
- proxy/kiro_region_test.go: TestResolveAccountRegionStableAndSpreads (same account -> same region always; many accounts -> multiple regions), TestResolveAccountRegionFallback (nil / empty-id / no-list -> non-empty global region), and TestEndpointChainCapped rewritten to assert single-region pinning (exactly 3 endpoints, no multi-region expansion).
- pool/least_request_test.go: the half-open single-probe test updated to the 2-slot floor (admits 2, sheds the 3rd with the saturation hint).
- go build, go vet, go test ./... all green.

Behavior notes: leaving KIRO_API_REGIONS unset keeps every account on the single default region (unchanged from before). A13 deliberately does NOT add any client-impersonation / fingerprint-masking; the region pinning is purely to keep each legitimately-held account's own traffic consistent and to spread load across accounts.

--- Adaptive load balancing — least-request default + per-account AIMD concurrency to curb 429 storms.

=== Why ===

Under bursty parallel load (agent fan-out / ultracode), the WHOLE account pool throttled at once. Each account is one AWS identity with its own per-identity token bucket, and SWRR balances assignment *rate* but is blind to *concurrency*: a simultaneous burst sprayed across all accounts before any 429 returned, so every bucket blew at the same moment, then recovered in lockstep and re-tripped. Logs showed 'throttled on all endpoints' across the pool with retry-after=0s.

=== Change — balance on outstanding load, not request count ===

- New DEFAULT strategy least-request (LOR): weighted least-outstanding-request selection, score = effectiveWeight/(inflight+1) (Envoy's form). swr / least-used / random remain selectable. GetPoolStrategy now defaults to least-request; the cfg==nil unit-test fallback stays swr so the existing SWRR tests hold.
- Per-account AIMD concurrency limit: +1 on a healthy success (gated on actually using the limit, capped at 12), x3/4 on a 429 (floored at 1) — self-tunes to AWS's unpublished bucket size.
- In-flight reservation: AcquireForModelExcluding reserves a slot under the lock and skips accounts at their limit; Release frees it (no-op for the other strategies). decayCountersLocked preserves entries with live inflight so a slot is never lost.
- Half-open recovery for free: the limit sits low (~1) after a cooldown, so only one probe is admitted until it succeeds.
- Jittered cooldown expiry (up to 3s) so a fleet that trips together doesn't recover on the same tick and re-stampede.
- Failover dispatcher (proxy/failover.go, proxy/quota.go) uses the reserving picker with a leak-safe Release defer, a bounded admission wait (2s) for a slot when saturated then sheds with Retry-After, and a tokenRefreshFailure sentinel so a token miss rotates without being misclassified as an upstream error.

Research basis: AWS exponential-backoff-and-jitter, Envoy least-request, Google SRE Handling Overload, Netflix concurrency-limits (AIMD).

=== Admin API + UI ===

- POST /admin/api/endpoint now accepts 'least-request' for poolStrategy (it was rejected by the validation allowlist even though the backend default resolves to it — the UI could not select the new default). swr / least-used / random unchanged.
- The Pool Strategy dropdown in admin Settings lists Least-Request first (default) with an expanded explanation; the JS default fallback is least-request.
- Per-account dashboard cards show a live 'In-flight' chip (outstanding / adaptive-limit) pushed over the status WebSocket, with EN/ZH i18n. dashboardSnapshot carries inflight + concurrencyLimit per account via the new pool.ConcurrencyState.

=== Tests ===

New dedicated unit tests for every new path: LOR weighted selection + saturation skip, AIMD grow/shrink math (RecordSuccess gated increase, RecordError x3/4 decrease, floor/cap), Acquire/Release accounting incl. leak-safety and decayCountersLocked preserving inflight, the half-open single-probe recovery, ConcurrencyState reporting, and (proxy) the saturation -> admission-wait -> shed path plus tokenRefreshFailure rotation. go build, go vet, go test ./... all green. Note: go test -race needs a working cgo toolchain (CI runs it on Linux); the dev box's race build is broken at the runtime/cgo step, so concurrency is covered by the explicit Acquire/Release accounting tests instead.

Behavior is reversible: setting poolStrategy back to swr fully disables the new concurrency path.

--- 1.0.10-A11 ---

Filter docs + FilterEnvNoise now defaults OFF so CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md reach the model.

=== Why ===

Claude Code does NOT send CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md in the request system field — it injects their content inside <system-reminder>...</system-reminder> blocks in the first user message. The FilterEnvNoise filter drops whole <system-reminder> blocks (to strip git status, environment dumps, and the reserved billing-header line), so with it ON the proxy was silently deleting the user's project memory files before they reached the upstream model. Confirmed at the filter layer: a CLAUDE.md instruction wrapped in <system-reminder> is stripped with FilterEnvNoise on and survives with it off, while the user's typed prompt is unaffected either way.

=== Change ===

- FilterEnvNoise now defaults OFF (was ON) for both fresh installs and the one-shot pre-existing-config migration. FilterClaudeCode and FilterStripBoundaries are unchanged (still default ON). Explicit operator toggles are still preserved — the FilterDefaultsApplied marker means an install that already set these is untouched.
- Tradeoff of the new default: CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md instructions are honored, at the cost of more tokens per request (env/git noise rides along) and loss of the billing-header strip. Operators who prefer the leaner prompt can re-enable FilterEnvNoise in admin Settings -> Built-in Filters.

=== Settings UI: detailed filter explanations ===

The three Built-in Filter hints in admin Settings were one-liners that didn't explain what each filter does, why it exists, or the tradeoff. All three are now expanded in both English and Chinese, each stating what it strips, its purpose, and a recommendation:
- 'Drop Claude Code system prompts' (filterClaudeCode): clarifies it targets the SYSTEM field only, and that CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md do NOT live there so this toggle does not affect them. Recommended ON.
- 'Strip environment noise lines' (filterEnvNoise): now states explicitly that CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md ride inside <system-reminder> blocks and that leaving this ON strips them; turn OFF to honor project memory files. Recommended ON only if you don't rely on CLAUDE.md.
- 'Clean boundary markers' (filterStripBoundaries): cosmetic, no effect on CLAUDE.md or tools, safe to leave ON.
The hints render via textContent, so the literal <system-reminder> text displays verbatim rather than being parsed as HTML.

Validation: go build, go vet, go test ./... all green; existing TestIronLawDroppedWhenFilterClaudeCodeOn still passes. Docs/default-only change — filter mechanics are unchanged. version.json and the compiled-in config.Version constant both bumped to 1.0.10-A11 so the dashboard badge, update check, and code agree.

Version-string sync + dashboard toolbar overflow fix.
